turns.content: Hey, what's the difference between hwlocality crate and raw syscalls for NUMA-aware allocation?

Good question! The hwlocality crate provides a high-level abstraction over the underlying libhwloc library, so you get safer and easier-to-use bindings. Raw syscalls are more low-level and give you fine-grained control but require careful error handling because they don't abstract over hardware differences as well.
Oh right, I thought hwlocality was just about locality aware scheduling, not allocation? That’s why I’m asking!

Ah, you're thinking of the libhwloc library's main focus on CPU affinity. While hwlocality crate exposes both, its primary strength is memory management: it lets you bind allocations to specific NUMA nodes for better locality and performance in HPC workloads.
So which one should I use? I want to allocate data close to where the CPU is running.

If you want simplicity and correctness, go with hwlocality. It wraps all the complexities of NUMA topology detection and binding for you. But if you need maximum control over every detail — say, setting memory policies on a per-thread basis or advanced affinity masks — then raw syscalls might be better.
